coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic mediated marine biofouling inhibition using nano CuO: TiO2-carbon dot embedded on organo silane surface modified polyethylene aquaculture cage nets

coredata.dc:description: Biofouling in aquaculture cages is a potential problem and its management become major concern to the planners and farmers. The study aimed to modify the surface of polyethylene aquaculture cage nets using organo silane and to evaluate the effectiveness of a nano CuO:TiO2-carbon dot biocide treatment in inhibiting marine biofouling through photocatalytic action. Polyethylene aquaculture cage net surface modified using organo silane and the surface embedded with biocide of nano CuO:TiO2 and carbon dot (CD) derived from fish eye. The biocide-treated surface was characterized using UV–Visible and Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometry. The organo silane interacts with polyethylene via hydrogen bonding, CuO:TiO2 interacts with the silane's Si, and the carbon dots' conjugated CC bonds interact with the transition metal and silane through van der Waals electrostatic forces and hydrogen bonding. Varied concentrations of CuO:TiO2 and CD was coated sequentially over silane surface modified polyethylene, exposed in the marine environment to evaluate biofouling inhibition efficiency and found 0.05% each of CuO:TiO2 and CD was optimum. The CuO:TiO2–CD coated polyethylene cage net tested for its biofouling inhibition for 8 months in marine environment and exhibited excellent biofouling inhibition. The inhibition of biofouling was attributed to the enhanced photocatalytic action, resulting from increased electron-hole recombination, thus generating ROS, O2٠, and OH٠ radicals. This led to the highest electronic activity around the cage net and also the formation of an acidic environment deterred microorganisms. The study highlighted the use of organo silane for surface modification of polyalkenes to load the biocide and also CuO:TiO2–CD is a potential biocide for biofouling inhibition in aquaculture cages.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic mediated marine biofouling inhibition using nano CuO: TiO2-carbon dot embedded on organo silane surface modified polyethylene aquaculture cage nets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ICAR Central Institute of Fisheries Technology, Matsyapuri PO, Cochin, 682 029, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: ICAR Central Institute of Fisheries Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biofouling in aquaculture cages is a potential problem and its management become major concern to the planners and farmers. The study aimed to modify the surface of polyethylene aquaculture cage nets using organo silane and to evaluate the effectiveness of a nano CuO:TiO2-carbon dot biocide treatment in inhibiting marine biofouling through photocatalytic action. Polyethylene aquaculture cage net surface modified using organo silane and the surface embedded with biocide of nano CuO:TiO2 and carbon dot (CD) derived from fish eye. The biocide-treated surface was characterized using UV–Visible and Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometry. The organo silane interacts with polyethylene via hydrogen bonding, CuO:TiO2 interacts with the silane's Si, and the carbon dots' conjugated CC bonds interact with the transition metal and silane through van der Waals electrostatic forces and hydrogen bonding. Varied concentrations of CuO:TiO2 and CD was coated sequentially over silane surface modified polyethylene, exposed in the marine environment to evaluate biofouling inhibition efficiency and found 0.05% each of CuO:TiO2 and CD was optimum. The CuO:TiO2–CD coated polyethylene cage net tested for its biofouling inhibition for 8 months in marine environment and exhibited excellent biofouling inhibition. The inhibition of biofouling was attributed to the enhanced photocatalytic action, resulting from increased electron-hole recombination, thus generating ROS, O2٠, and OH٠ radicals. This led to the highest electronic activity around the cage net and also the formation of an acidic environment deterred microorganisms. The study highlighted the use of organo silane for surface modification of polyalkenes to load the biocide and also CuO:TiO2–CD is a potential biocide for biofouling inhibition in aquaculture cages.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Accumulation of foulers over marine exposed structures is an important issue and the remedial measures mainly focused on biocide based coating. Biofouling causes significant economic and environmental risks to stakeholders. Biofouling typically initialized with microorganisms colonizing exposed materials in aquatic environments, followed by macro organisms such as animals and plants (Tian et al., 2021). Aquaculture industry is coming up extensively in India since their ability to produce cheaper fish based protein, lively hood improvement and increased export potential. Aquaculture cages are heavily affected by biofouling, as these foulers block the lumens of the cages, thereby production affected due to the reduced oxygen and nutrient circulation in the cages (Fitridge et al., 2012). Management measures to remove fouler from cages costs more than 25% of the project cost (Braithwaite et al., 2007).

Current antifouling strategies employed for submerged structures are based on copper oxide release coatings, tributyltin, self polishing polymers, fouling release coatings, low surface energy polymers, and amphiphilic and hydrophilic polymers (Wang et al., 2023). None of these methodologies will be efficient for aquaculture cage nets, primarily composed of polyethylene or polypropylene. Due to the non-polar nature of polyalkenes, treating cage nets with biocides poses a challenge. Ashraf et al. (2020) developed a methodology to transform non polar surface of PE to polar one by in situ synthesising of polyaniline (PANI), a conducting polymer, over the PE. Surface modified PE was loaded with nano biocides, such as copper oxide, and the results exhibited excellent biofouling resistance both in the laboratory setting and under field conditions (Ashraf et al., 2022a). The biocide, nano copper oxide, acts as tiny islands in the matrix and inhibit the attachment of micro organisms and thereby macro foulers. The major disadvantage of this technology was the cumbersome process for PANI surface modification and wastage of unused chemicals. Evaluation of the mentioned PE-PANI- CuO treated cage net in the aquaculture farmers's field, with the exception of low resistance against exotic bivalves of the Mytilidae species. These reasons made low adoption of the technology by the farmers, indicating the need to develop more efficient biofouling resistant aquaculture cage nets.

Functionalized textile fibres are getting attention in recent years. Among many additives organo silane based sol gel materials desired since its treatment improved chemical and physical properties of fabrics (Sfameni et al., 2022a). Super hydrophobic surfaces were developed Richard et al. (2013), and their importance was highlighted its unique water repellant, ice formation resistant, anti sticking and self cleaning behaviours (Xu et al., 2010). The organo silane molecules were used to modify the silica surfaces to load the enzymes, and to fabricate hydrophobic coating (Manna et al., 2023; Chu et al., 1997; W Xing et al., 2012). Application of alkyl(trialkoxy)silane were advantageous since its low price, non toxic (Plutino et al., 2017; Ielo et al., 2020; Sfameni et al., 2022b), and expected to be a viable alternative for polyaniline coating over PE.

Not many studies have been conducted to modify polymeric hydrocarbons like PE or similar polymer surfaces using silane-based materials, despite its extensive use in the textile sector. Cotton textile materials are non-polar, and organo silanes have demonstrated excellent adhesion over textile surfaces. Effort to modify the surface of PE with an organo silane will be easier than polyaniline. The hydrophobic surfaces formed solely due to the treatment of silane cannot be directly employed for antifouling in a marine environment. Therefore, fabrication of a semi hydrophilic and hydrophobic surface expected to give efficient biofouling inhibition (Rahimi et al., 2024). The biocides like CuO or ZnO exhibited as hydrophilic character and their treatment over hydrophobic silane coated surface will exhibit semi hydrophilic or hydrophobic behaviour with an efficient biofouling resistance (Mohan and Ashraf, 2019; Popescu et al., 2011; Rahimi et al., 2024). Copper is a historically known biocide and proved its anti bacterial, anti viral, biofouling resistance in several studies (Ashraf et al., 2022a; Naseer et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2013). Similarly, titanium dioxide, which exists in different crystallographic format, is a well utilized antibacterial molecule. Nano titanium dioxide is not only an excellent and widely used photocatalytic agent with antibacterial advantages, high chemical stability, smaller size with a high specific surface area, and lower harmful effects, but it also exhibits a large band gap under UV environments (Gyorgyey et al., 2016; Lei et al., 2015; Yadav et al., 2016; Dong et al., 2020a). Photocatalytic property with microbial inhibition and removal of Cr (VI) from water was achieved by employing Cu2O@TiO2@viscose composite (Alaysuy et al., 2023). Potential of nano TiO2 as biocide against marine biofouling is not much explored.

Titanium dioxide is a promising photocatalytic semiconductor with limitation of wide band gap and hence required UV radiation for activation (Liu et al., 2019; Arfanis et al., 2019). Several authors attempted to improve the TiO2 characteristics like photocatalytic activity, creation of electron –holes and extension of their separation time, by employing different techniques like doping (Wu et al., 2019), surface modification (Sun et al., 2019), and fabricating composites using semiconductor metal oxides (Rekeb et al., 2019; Park et al., 2020). Nano CuO is a preferred choice due to its band gap energy of 2.1 eV (Rekeb et al., 2019), its status as a p-type semiconductor, its thermostability, low cost, less toxic, status as an essential micronutrient, good optical and catalytic activity, and high activity in the visible spectrum (Wang et al., 2020; Shaislamov et al., 2020; Hamad et al., 2022). The photo degradation efficiency of TiO2 with methylene blue was 13% but it increased to 85% when a CuO–TiO2 composite was used (Shawky et al., 2024). CuO–TiO2 nano composite successfully degraded rhodamine dye, methyl orange, and methylene blue under visible light (Bathla et al., 2020; Bae and Jang, 2019). Biological and green chemistry approaches are considered to be safer method to produce nano composites. Shawky et al. (2024) prepared a ternary composite of CuO–TiO2–Ag, which exhibited increased photocatalytic activity with higher dye degradation efficiency. The role of silver was to facilitate charge separation and transfer through the interfaces of the p-n heterojunction of CuO–TiO2, aligned with the created Schottky junction by silver (Zhang et al., 2016). In another study a ternary composite using Pt/ZnO/MWCNT showed a 74% photocatalytic efficiency in degradation of methylene blue (Sobahi et al., 2017). This implies that carbon analogues can also be utilized to improve the heterojunctions and stabilise electron – hole pairs. Nano titanium oxide coated copper exhibited increased light harvest, photocatalytic activity, super hydrophilicity and biofouling inhibition (Liu et al., 2016). Several graphene oxide based hybrids were fabricated by combining with semiconductor metal oxides, had synergistically influenced to improve the photocatalytic activity, degradation of organic dyes and pathogenic inhibition (Majumder and Gangopadhyay, 2022). Increased surface area, selective adsorption, and charge separation of graphene oxide complemented the formed GO-MO hybrids to their effective application in water purification, disease diagnosis, drug release, sensors and removal of pollutants (Majumder and Gangopadhyay, 2022). Photocatalytic efficiency is depends on the formation of CuO–TiO2 p-n heterojunction, charge carrier production, and life time (Wang et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2017). The photo generated electron approach towards the bulk, where as electron move towards the interface. Introduction of carbon dot in TiO2/CuO/Carbon dot improved the charge transfer, e/h + recombination and photo generated charges (Yadeta and Imae, 2023). Fahri et al. (2022) demonstrated that TiO2/CuO/carbon dot composite having higher electromagnetic wave absorbing efficiency, and photocatalytic degradation of fipronil pesticide. The above studies specifically highlighted the role of carbon nanotubes, graphene and its analogues, and carbon dot for improving photocatalytic activity in presence of metal oxide composites. Even though TiO2/CuO/CD composite was used for varied applications by exploiting the improved photocatalytic effect, but their potential was not used for combating biofouling in the aquatic environments.

Herein the present study demonstrated a facile method to modify the surface of polyethylene aquaculture cage net using organo silane molecule. This enables the sequentially loading of the biocide nano CuO:TiO2 and CD onto the polyethylene to inhibit marine biofouling on aquaculture cage nets. The biocide enhanced the photocatalytic activity in the vicinity of treated nets resulted excellent biofouling resistance in long term. The present study aimed to modify the PE surface using (3-glycidyloxypropyl)trimethoxysilane and to incorporate the biocides to the modified surface and its evaluation against biofouling resistance. The biocide employed here is a combination of nano CuO and TiO2 and fish eye derived CD. The nano material coated aquaculture cage net biofouling resistance is evaluated under marine environment.

(3-glycidyloxypropyl)trimethoxysilane (hereafter known as Silane), was procured from Sigma Aldrich, nano copper oxide (40 nm size, 99% purity and 80 m2 g−1specific area), and nano titanium oxide (∼7 nm size, 95% purity and 326 m2 g−1 specific area), were purchased from SRL Chemicals India. Blue coloured aquaculture cage net made of high density polyethylene was purchased from Government owned Matsyafed Net Factory, Ernakulam. Milli Q type 1 water was used throughout the analysis. Carbon dot derived from Rohu fish eye was having size 5 nm, UV–Vis spectral absorption at 225 and 263 nm and the fluorescence emission maxima was ranged from 431 to 466 nm for excitation wavelength of 220–400 nm (paper communicated).

The aquaculture cage nets were cleaned by using dilute HCl solution followed by sonicated by immersing in the acetone to remove grease and dirts. The aquaculture cage net was treated with 0.02 M silane by immersing the net for 3 h and dried in an air oven at 60 °C for 2 h. Preliminary evaluation carried out by treating the silane treated net with a solution having varied ratios of nano CuO:TiO2 mixture and its spectrophotometric evaluation revealed that the optimum nano CuO: TiO2 mixture ratio was 1:2. A mixture of nano CuO:TiO2 (1:2) was prepared. From this mixture varied concentrations of nano CuO:TiO2 was taken based on the treatments. The silane treated cage net was immersed in the CuO:TiO2 solution over night after bath sonication. The treated nets were taken out and allowed to dry in air. The nano CuO:TiO2 treated cage net was immersed in 0.05% nano carbon dot (CD) derived from fish eye solution for over night. The details of (CuO:TiO2):CD treatments were given below. V0 untreated PE control, V1 Silane treated PE, V2 Silane treated PE+0.05% CD, V3 Silane treated PE +0.01% (nano CuO:TiO2) +0.05% CD, V4 Silane treated PE+0.02% (nano CuO:TiO2) +0.05% CD, V5 Silane treated PE+0.05% (nano CuO:TiO2) +0.05% CD. The Treated aquaculture cage nets was dried in air, pre-weighed and tagged with tokens. These net samples were tied over a square plastic frame and immersed in the test site of Cochin estuary at 1 m below the low tide height for 3 months. Due to the paucity of space in the test site, worried about the uniformity of exposure (depth within 0.75–1.25 m) and forced to keep the sample racks in horizontal position prevented to keep more replication of the treated net. After three months exposure the best among the treatments were repeated with replications and this data was used for discussion.

The exposed samples were retrieved in varied month intervals. The samples were cleaned to remove the adsorbed dirts using running water, air dried and the dry weights were recorded using 0.001 g sensitive electronic balance. The extent of biomass accumulation was studied by examining the net through Leica MZ16 stereo microscope in varied magnification. Scanning electron microscope (JEOL Model JSM – 6390LV) was used to surface characterize the treated cage nets after gold sputtering. Fourier transform infrared spectra of treated cage net was analysed by scanning from 650 to 4000 cm−1 using Thermo Nicolet Si10 FTIR fitted with Zn–Se iTR accessory. Average spectra of 50 numbers of scan recorded and the resulting data was analysed using Thermo Nicolet Omnic software.

The aquaculture cage nets were treated as per the treatment details described in the materials and method section. The samples were tied over high density PVC tube frames and exposed in the Cochin estuary for 8 months. On visual observation the CuO:TiO2 were embedded in the PE-silane surface.

UV–visible spectrophotometric evaluation of PE, silane treated PE (PE-silane), PE-silane-nano CuO:TiO2 and PE-silane-nano CuO:TiO2-CD were carried out and the results shown in Fig. 1 and Supplementary file Table S1. The untreated PE exhibited absorption at 740, 676, 400, 350, 275 and 197 nm. The PE-silane exhibited additional absorptions at 608, 573, 536 and 340 nm with slight shifts in the absorptions compared to PE absorption indicated that silane was attached successfully over the net. Further there was a clear red shift in absorption in the UV region. The PE-silane -CuO:TiO2 coated cage net was showed an additional absorptions at 726, 506, and 324 nm compared to PE-silane. The absorption wavelength showed in PE-Silane was 406, 362, 340, 279 nm and shifted respectively to 411, 356, 321, 299 nm in PE-silane-CuO:TiO2 cage net samples. The additional absorption and shift in wavelength compared to PE-silane clearly indicated the presence of the CuO and TiO2 in the matrix. The PE-silane-CuO:TiO2-CD treated aquaculture cage net exhibited additional absorptions at 612, and 243 nm. Red shift was exhibited by the absorption wavelength of 321, 299 and 324 nm in PE-silane CuO:TiO2 coated net respectively to 332, 304, and 264 nm. This showed that the CD was incorporated in the matrix. Previous studies showed that CD π – π* transition exhibited around 220 nm and other functional groups were around 270 nm (Ashraf et al., 2022b). Here the CD was adsorbed over PE-silane- CuO–TiO2 was through the interaction of conjugated double bond as evidenced by the red shift of the CD specific π – π* absorption from 220 to 243 nm with low intensity, which highlights the utilisation of π bonds for adsorption with bands of CuO and TiO2.
Scanning electron micrographs of silane coated PE cage net and nano CuO:TiO2-CD coated net was shown in Fig. 2 . The CD, nano CuO:TiO2 were distributed uniformly in the silane treated matrix.

FTIR analysis results of PE-silane, PE-silane-CuO:TiO2, PE-silane-CuO:TiO2-CD was shown in Fig. 3 and their FTIR absorption details with functional groups given in Table 1 . The FTIR absorption by Si–O–C was exhibited at 1137, 1147, and 1157 cm−1 respectively in PE-silane, PE-silane-CuO:TiO2, and PE-silane-CuO:TiO2-CD. Introduction of CuO:TiO2 and CD had showed shift in FTIR absorption frequencies compared to PE-silane indicated the strain experienced over the Si-(OCH3)3. Similarly C–O–C epoxy absorption was showed a shift from 1244 to 1238 cm−1 when CuO:TiO2 was introduced. As stated the strain over Si–O–C was experienced due to the presence of CuO:TiO2 and CuO:TiO2-CD was further emphasised by shifting CH3 absorption from 1387 to 1375 cm−1. Three CH2 str vibration frequencies exhibited from 1440 to 1475 cm−1 and there was a slight shift in frequencies due to the addition of CuO:TiO2 and CuO:TiO2-CD but the intensity of absorption was different especially in the latter. On examining the ratios of intensity of absorption at 1461 and 1471 cm−1 showed 1.172, 1.255 and 1.378 respectively for PE-silane, PE-silane-CuO:TiO2 and PE-silane-CuO:TiO2-CD. This showed that addition of CD had contributed more CH2 and CH3 in the matrix indicating that CD was attached over the PE-silane-CuO:TiO2 system. Also absorption frequencies exclusively related to CC conjugated and aromatic, and two CO (ketone and carboxylic) form also showed in PE-silane-CuO:TiO2-CD coated net clearly indicated the strong presence of CD in the matrix. Oxide absorption from silane underwent significant shift (2635–2735 cm−1) in PE-silane-CuO:TiO2-CD treated net compared to PE-silane-CuO:TiO2. Comparative evaluation of C–H str vibration of CH2 and CH3 from 2800 to 3000 cm−1 region underwent significant shifts in PE-silane-CuO:TiO2-CD than PE-silane-CuO:TiO2. Hydrogen bonded O–H str were exhibited at two environments and can be explained the interaction between PE, with silane, CuO:TiO2 and CD. The FTIR data revealed that Si was interacted with CuO:TiO2 and CD interaction mainly through conjugated CC double bond with transition metal electron clouds and silane organics.

Water wettability was correlated with the measure of contact angle and here we just observed the wettability in different PE film surfaces by dispensing 10 μl of water and viewed under stereo microscope at 15× magnification. Surface wetting of PE, PE-silane, PE-silane-CuO:TiO2, PE-silane-CuO:TiO2-CD using tap water and seawater under a stereo microscope was shown in Fig. 4 . The water and seawater over PE was exhibited hydrophobic characteristics. The silane coated PE exhibited more spread of water in both the cases and seawater showed higher wetting indicated that higher surface free energy. Similarly when nano CuO:TiO2 was introduced over the PE-silane surface the water drops showed more agglomerated or beaded up, implied that the surface was more hydrophobic than silane coated surface and seawater drop showed more agglomeration. Introduction of nano CuO and TiO2 exhibited more hydrophobic than silane surface was due to the influence of two nano sizes related roughness patterns variation, fractal roughness and less favoured hydrogen bonding minimized the contact area (Bhattacharya et al., 2008; Bhushan et al., 2009). When CD was introduced over nano CuO:TiO2 surface the droplet showed higher spread, indicating that CD treatment reduced contact angle than PE alone. Here the CD occupied the nano rough surface created by the CuO:TiO2 resulted the water wetting the surface due to the hydrogen bonding and Wenzel state formation. Similar results were reported when graphene oxide introduced over Ti surface (Yaghoubi and Foroutan, 2020). The results clearly established that the surface becomes partially hydrophobic/hydrophilic behaviour and hence expected higher efficiency in biofouling inhibition than pure hydrophobic or hydrophilic panels (Rahimi et al., 2024).

The PE cage nets treated with silane, silane-CuO:TiO2 and silane-CuO:TiO2-CD (6 treatments) as described in the materials and method section. The treated samples were exposed to the estuarine environment up to 3 months to identify the most efficient among the treatments (Supplementary Fig. S1 and Fig. S2). Initial study was conducted without any replication due to the paucity of space in the test site. One month exposed samples showed treatment V2 (PE-silane +0.05%CD) has highest biomass accumulation and V5 (PE-silane-0.05%(CuO:TiO)-0.05% CD) was the lowest. In the 3 month sample the highest biomass accumulated in the V3 (PE-silane-0.01% (CuO:TiO2)-0.05% CD) treatment and lowest was in the V5. The biomass accumulation during the marine exposure studies for untreated polyethylene, PE-silane, PE-silane-CuO:TiO2-CD respectively during 1 month exposure was 0.268, 0.313 and 0.236 g g−1 net. For three month exposed samples the biomass accumulation was 0.295, 0.198 and 0.192 g g−1 net respectively in untreated polyethylene, PE-silane, and PE-silane-CuO:TiO2-CD. The V5 treatment with 0.05% each of CuO:TiO2 and CD exhibited excellent biofouling resistance compared to other treatments. Microscopic evaluation results were also correlated with the above findings and shown in the Supplementary Fig. S2. Silane+0.05% CD comparatively higher biomass accumulation both sampling periods indicated that introduction of CD alone cannot reduce the biofouling. In V3 and V4 the CuO:TiO2 mixture was 0.01 and 0.02% was not influenced much on biofouling inhibition. 0.05% each of CD and CuO:TiO2 exhibited highest biofouling resistance among all treatments. Here all the three components were 0.05% and all the components synergistically influenced the biofouling resistance. To test the long term efficiency of V5 treatment against biofouling resistance a detailed experiments were conducted by exposing the samples in the estuarine environment.

The untreated PE and PE-silane-0.05% (CuO:TiO2)-0.05% CD (V5) treated cage net was exposed in the Cochin estuary during February 2023. The samples were retrieved after 1, 2, 4, 6 and 8 months intervals. Fig. 5 showed the stereo microscopic images of the biomass accumulation pattern in treated and untreated panels in varied months. Hydroids were completely covered the meshes of control samples whereas treated showed not much biomass attachment in one month samples. The premonsoon season in the region exhibited salinity of 31.07 psμ (sampled on 16 March 2023), dissolved oxygen of 2.8 mg l−1and pH 7.80. One month samples exhibited severe attack of an exotic bivalve named Mytillae strigata. The cage net holding frame was targeted by the spores of mytilidae species initially, while it grown, slowly extended to near side experimental nets as evidenced by its accumulation to the sample near to the frame. It was found that organism attached more over the untreated PE and partial over the treated net. The treated cage net overcomes the spontaneous attack of mytilidae species with 22% efficiency. Mytillae strigata is an exotic species recently reported in the region, with spontaneous eruption and severe accumulation has major issue among aquaculture farmers (Ravinesh et al., 2023). Mostly field experiments were failed due to unpredicted growth of these species and the phenomenon had made difficult to understand the role of regular biofoulers in our treatments. Similar behaviour was seen samples retrieved after two months. Again 4 and 6 month samples are retrieved during monsoon season June and August respectively and these months experiences heavy rain fall in the region (monsoon season) (Thomas et al., 2021). Increased inflow of fresh water due to rain and river discharge in the area led to sloughing off the mytilidae species from the exposed net. The salinity was dropped to 6.22 and 12.12 psμ on 4 and 6 month respectively and the pH was 7.16 and 7.48. The samples were accumulated with algal type biofoulers over the net and both the cases treated one fared well. 8 month sample retrieved during the month of October 2023 and this period was immediately after monsoon. The panels exhibited fresh more algal accumulation in the treated sample whereas hydroid like foulers and green algae dominated in the control samples. The results showed that biofouling inhibition by the CuO:TiO-CD composite was efficient in varied seasons, and fouling density in varied seasons The results clearly emphasised the surface modification of PE with silane also a good option and the nano CuO:TiO2 and CD synergistically influenced the biofouling inhibition. Surface modification of polyethylene with organo silane was easier, less labour intensive process and efficient compared to the polyaniline surface modification reported Ashraf et al. (2020).

The cage net containing 3 different active ingredients and its synergistic influence reduced the biofouling in the aquaculture cage nets. First individual actions of three components can be analysed. It has already reported that the mechanism of action by carbon dot to bacterial cell wall was due to the electrostatic interaction (Dong et al., 2020b). CD is an excellent light harvester under UV and IR radiation and also good photodynamic properties (Ge et al., 2014, 2016; Juzenas et al., 2013; Kleinauskas et al., 2013; Lan et al., 2018). Reactive oxygen species [ROS] production due to the presence of CD will damage the cell membrane and the DNA/RNA, which result erratic signals from genes (Al Awak et al., 2017; Li et al., 2016, 2018). CD interaction with bacterial cell in presence of light, water and air will produce ROS, OH free radicals and singlet oxygen. These reactive components will result the cell damage and death of organism due to necrosis/apoptosis (Ipe et al., 2005; Jhonsi et al., 2018). Li et al. (2016) described the antibacterial influence by the positively charged CD-spermidine composite interact with negatively charged cell of the microorganism.

CuO and TiO2 are considered as p and n type conductors respectively (Pelaez et al., 2012). CuO has narrow band gap (1.2–1.5 eV), good electrical, thermal characteristics, non toxic, cheaper, high quantum efficiency, sustainable and minimum electron hole recombination (Zhang et al., 2014; Iqbal et al., 2021; Bahadur et al., 2018, 2021; Yentür and Dükkan, 2020). TiO2 loaded CuO is known photocatalyst under visible light (Kubiak et al., 2020; De Brito and Zanoni, 2017), here the light induced excitation of valence band electron to conductive band of CuO, and transferred to TiO2 conductive band (Li et al., 2008). Expected lag time of excited charge carrier recombination in CuO–TiO2 during photo catalysis results higher quantum efficiency (Yu et al., 2015).

Altin (2022) described the mechanism of CuO–TiO2- graphene oxide on degradation of bisphenol A. The band gap energy of CuO–TiO2 was 3.07 eV and CuO–TiO2-GO was 3.05 eV and the latter's photocatalytic activity mechanism was explained by valence band electron of CuO excited to conduction band resulted the initialisation of photocatalytic reaction by generating electron pairs e−/h+. These electron pairs transferred to the CuO–TiO2-GO surface and undergo redox reaction (Cheng et al., 2014). Here graphene acts as electron acceptor and carrier due to its high specific surface area and conductivity (Sun et al., 2015). These photo generated electrons interact with CuO–TiO2-GO adsorbed oxygen and water and respectively form O2٠ And OH٠ radicals. These radicals were responsible for photo degradation of BPA. The present study the nano CD was used instead of graphene, CD is also having good conductivity. CD interaction with the microorganism in presence of light, air, and water also initialise the production of ROS, OH٠ and singlet oxygen (Ipe et al., 2005; Jhonsi et al., 2018). Introduction of CD to the composite enhanced the wider light absorption range, reduced the speed of recombination, increased the reactive oxygen species generation and inherent antibacterial property of CD (Song et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2023). These together played to inhibit the formation biofilm formation and hence biofouling resistance. Introduction of CuO with TiO2 enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency through electron – hole e/h + recombination, production of ROS and free radicals. Addition of CD with CuO:TiO2 improves the surface making it semi hydrophilic/phobic, which deters protein adsorption, expands the light absorption range, and inherently inhibits algae and biofilm-forming microorganisms. These synergistic influences make the composite more effective in inhibiting biofouling.

The surface of polyethylene aquaculture cage net successfully coated with organo silane. UV–visible and FTIR characterisation revealed the silane interacted with polyethylene through hydrogen bonding, the CuO:TiO2 interacted with Si of silane and finally the conjugated CC of CD interacted with the transition metal and silane through Van der waals electrostatic forces and hydrogen bonding. Optimum concentration of biocide (CuO:TiO2) and CD was 0.05% each, which exhibited the highest biofouling inhibition during an eight-month exposure to the marine environment. The biocide showed improved photocatalytic activity by increased e−/h + recombination.
